Online courses often have a far higher attrition rate than equivalent face-to-face offerings.
words.englishDefinition: the proportion of enrolled students who withdraw or drop out before completing a programme

exampleSentences: She registered to audit the course, attending every lecture without submitting assignments or receiving a grade.
Retired professionals frequently audit courses at local universities simply to stay intellectually engaged.
words.englishDefinition: a class attended for learning purposes only, without earning credit or formal assessment

words.reading: バチェラーズディグリー
exampleSentences: Many entry-level positions in finance now require a bachelor's degree as a minimum qualification.

words.englishDefinition: an undergraduate academic degree typically awarded after three to four years of study at a university

words.reading: ボードイグザミネイション
exampleSentences: All medical graduates must pass the national board examination before they are licensed to practise.

words.englishDefinition: a standardised licensing exam administered by a professional or government body to certify competence in a field

exampleSentences: The capstone project required students to design and present a solution to a real community problem.

words.englishDefinition: a culminating project or experience that integrates and demonstrates the skills gained throughout a programme of study

words.reading: ケースベースドラーニング
exampleSentences: Case-based learning is standard in law and business schools, requiring students to analyse real-world scenarios.

distractors: コードスイッチング（言語切り替え）

exampleSentences: The charter school operates independently of the district but must meet the state's academic performance standards.
Supporters argue that charter schools introduce competitive pressure that raises standards across all local schools.
words.englishDefinition: a publicly funded but independently operated school granted autonomy from some district regulations in exchange for accountability

words.reading: クラスパーティシペイション
exampleSentences: Class participation accounted for twenty percent of the final grade, incentivising students to speak up.

words.reading: クラスルームマネジメント
exampleSentences: Effective classroom management minimises disruptions and ensures that instructional time is used productively.

words.englishDefinition: the techniques and strategies a teacher uses to maintain an orderly, productive learning environment

exampleSentences: The school's co-curriculum — debate, drama, and robotics clubs — complemented its rigorous academic programme.
Universities increasingly market co-curriculum opportunities as part of the overall student development experience.
words.englishDefinition: organised educational activities outside formal lessons that complement and reinforce the academic curriculum

exampleSentences: A cognitivist perspective frames learning as the active construction of mental schemas, not passive absorption.
The course design reflected a cognitivist philosophy, emphasising problem-solving and metacognitive awareness.
words.englishDefinition: relating to the theory that learning involves internal mental processes such as thinking, memory, and problem-solving

words.reading: カレッジプレパラトリー
exampleSentences: The college preparatory curriculum exposed students to AP courses and standardised test strategies from tenth grade.

words.reading: コミュニティカレッジ
words.jpTranslation: コミュニティカレッジ
exampleSentences: She earned her associate degree at a community college before transferring to a four-year university.

words.englishDefinition: a two-year public institution offering associate degrees and vocational training, often as a pathway to four-year universities

words.reading: コンピテンシーベースト
exampleSentences: The competency-based nursing programme allows students to progress as soon as they demonstrate each clinical skill.

words.reading: コンストラクティヴィズム
exampleSentences: Constructivism holds that learners build new knowledge by connecting it to prior experience rather than absorbing it passively.
Piaget's constructivism profoundly influenced how primary school teachers design hands-on learning activities.
words.englishDefinition: a learning theory asserting that individuals actively construct knowledge through experience and reflection rather than receiving it passively

words.reading: コンティニュイングエデュケイション
exampleSentences: Lawyers in this jurisdiction must complete forty hours of continuing education annually to maintain their licence.

words.englishDefinition: formal learning pursued by professionals after initial qualification to update or expand their knowledge and skills

words.reading: コウオペラティヴラーニング
exampleSentences: Cooperative learning tasks assigned each group member a distinct role, ensuring everyone contributed meaningfully.

distractors: 周辺化された人々、サバルタン
words.englishDefinition: an instructional strategy in which students work together in structured small groups to achieve shared learning goals

exampleSentences: The university's core curriculum requires all students to take courses in writing, quantitative reasoning, and ethics.
Debate over the core curriculum centred on whether Western literary classics should remain mandatory.
distractors: 分散コンピューティング
words.englishDefinition: the set of compulsory subjects all students must study, regardless of their chosen specialisation

exampleSentences: She reduced her course load after her research supervisor warned that burnout would compromise her dissertation.
International students on a visa must maintain a minimum course load to remain compliant with immigration rules.
words.englishDefinition: the total number of courses or credit hours a student is enrolled in during a given term

exampleSentences: Most bachelor's programmes require 120 credit hours, typically spread across four years of full-time study.
Each credit hour generally corresponds to one hour of classroom instruction plus two hours of independent study per week.
words.englishDefinition: a unit measuring the academic workload associated with a course, typically based on weekly hours of instruction

words.reading: クリティカルペダゴジー
exampleSentences: Inspired by Freire, critical pedagogy encourages students to question the social structures that shape their lives.
The department adopted critical pedagogy to ensure that marginalised voices were centred in classroom discussions.
distractors: オーケストレーション
words.englishDefinition: an educational philosophy that encourages learners to critically examine social power structures and challenge injustice

words.reading: クロスディシプリナリー
exampleSentences: The cross-disciplinary seminar brought together historians, economists, and climate scientists to address environmental policy.
Her cross-disciplinary research drew on neuroscience, linguistics, and education theory simultaneously.
words.englishDefinition: spanning or combining insights from multiple academic disciplines

words.reading: カリキュラムアライメント
exampleSentences: Curriculum alignment ensures that what teachers teach, how they teach it, and how they assess it are mutually consistent.
After the national standards were updated, school districts undertook curriculum alignment reviews across all grade levels.
words.englishDefinition: the process of matching instructional content, teaching methods, and assessments to established learning standards or goals

words.reading: カリキュラムディザイン
exampleSentences: Effective curriculum design sequences content so that foundational skills precede advanced concepts.
The committee overhauled curriculum design to better align with national standards and workplace demands.
words.englishDefinition: the structured planning of what, how, and in what order subjects or skills are taught in an educational program

words.reading: ディグリープログラム
exampleSentences: She switched from a part-time degree program to full-time study after receiving a scholarship.
The university launched a degree program in data science to meet surging industry demand.

words.englishDefinition: a formal course of study at a university that leads to the award of an academic qualification

exampleSentences: Without digital literacy, many adults struggle to evaluate the credibility of online health information.
The government funded workshops to raise digital literacy among elderly residents in rural areas.
words.englishDefinition: the ability to use, evaluate, and create information using digital technologies safely and effectively

words.reading: ドクトラルプログラム
exampleSentences: Completing a doctoral program typically requires original research culminating in a peer-reviewed dissertation.

words.englishDefinition: a university course of study at the highest academic level, leading to a doctorate or PhD

words.reading: デュアルエンロールメント

words.englishDefinition: a system in which a student is simultaneously registered at two educational institutions, typically high school and college

words.reading: アーリーチャイルドフッド
exampleSentences: Neuroscientists confirm that early childhood is the most critical window for language acquisition.
Investment in early childhood education yields higher returns than intervention at any later stage of development.
words.englishDefinition: the developmental period from birth to approximately eight years old

words.reading: エデュケイショナルエクウィティ

Policymakers argue that closing the achievement gap requires a genuine commitment to educational equity.
words.englishDefinition: the principle that every student receives the resources and support needed to reach their full potential, regardless of background

words.reading: エデュケイショナルアウトカム
exampleSentences: Longitudinal studies link early bilingualism to measurably stronger educational outcomes in adolescence.
Schools are judged on educational outcomes such as literacy rates and post-secondary enrollment, not just test scores.
words.englishDefinition: a measurable result of an educational process, such as a skill gained or qualification achieved

words.reading: エデュケイショナルテクノロジー

Critics warn that over-reliance on educational technology may widen the digital divide for under-resourced schools.
words.englishDefinition: the use of digital tools, platforms, and media to facilitate and enhance teaching and learning

exampleSentences: She chose philosophy as her elective course to complement a compulsory economics major.

distractors: メタボリックシンドローム
words.englishDefinition: an optional subject that students choose freely, in addition to required core subjects

words.reading: エマージングリテラシー

words.reading: エンピリカルリサーチ
exampleSentences: The phonics debate was eventually settled by empirical research comparing outcomes across thousands of classrooms.

words.englishDefinition: research that draws conclusions from direct observation or experiment rather than theory alone

words.reading: エンリッチメントプログラム
exampleSentences: The museum's summer enrichment program exposed gifted students to archaeology and ancient language studies.

words.reading: エクウィティインエデュケイション

words.reading: エヴィデンスベースト

words.reading: イグザムプレパレイション

words.reading: イクスピアリエンシャルラーニング
exampleSentences: Sailing across the Pacific was, for the crew of student researchers, the ultimate experiential learning journey.

words.reading: エクストラカリキュラー

words.reading: ファカルティデヴェロップメント

words.reading: フィールドプレイスメント

words.reading: フォーマティヴアセスメント

exampleSentences: Several Nordic countries offer free tuition to domestic and EU students at public universities.

exampleSentences: She received a full scholarship covering tuition, accommodation, and a monthly stipend for four years.

words.reading: ファンクショナルリテラシー

exampleSentences: Although learning style theories are popular, robust research shows little evidence they should dictate instruction.

exampleSentences: Students who practice metacognition are better at recognising when they do not truly understand the material.

words.reading: マルティカルチャラルエデュケイション

words.reading: パフォーマンスタスク

words.reading: パーソナライズドラーニング

words.reading: プロブレムベースドラーニング

words.reading: プロフェッショナルデヴェロップメント

exampleSentences: Students who failed the placement test were enrolled in a remediation course before joining the regular cohort.

words.reading: リサーチメソドロジー
exampleSentences: The committee rejected the proposal because the research methodology conflated correlation with causation.

exampleSentences: A toxic school culture can undermine even the most carefully designed curriculum reforms.

words.reading: セルフダイレクテッドラーニング

exampleSentences: Through service learning, students tutored refugees while writing reflective journals linked to their sociology coursework.

words.reading: ソウシャルエモウショナルラーニング

words.reading: スチューデントセンタード

words.reading: サブジェクトマターエクスパート

words.reading: コンパウンドグロウス

words.reading: コンシューマープライスインデックス

words.reading: コストオブリヴィング

システマティックレビュー

exampleSentences: Telemedicine enables psychiatrists to reach patients in remote towns where no specialist clinic exists.
