words.englishDefinition: a statistical technique that examines relationships among three or more variables simultaneously

exampleSentences: Gender and nationality are examples of nominal data because they represent categories, not quantities.
Nominal data cannot be ranked or subjected to arithmetic operations, limiting the statistical tests available.
words.englishDefinition: categorical data that can be sorted into groups without any inherent order or numerical value

words.jpTranslation: ノンパラメトリック検定
exampleSentences: The researcher chose a non-parametric test because the data were not normally distributed.
Non-parametric tests are more appropriate for small samples or ordinal data than parametric alternatives.
words.englishDefinition: a statistical test that does not assume the data follow a particular distribution

exampleSentences: The null hypothesis stated that there would be no significant difference between the two groups.
A p-value below 0.05 is conventionally used as grounds to reject the null hypothesis.
words.englishDefinition: the default assumption in hypothesis testing that there is no effect or relationship between variables

exampleSentences: Observational research in schools showed that peer interactions peaked during unstructured activity time.
Because observational research does not involve manipulation, causal conclusions must be drawn cautiously.
words.englishDefinition: a study in which the researcher watches and records behaviour without intervening or manipulating variables

exampleSentences: Researchers must clarify their ontological assumptions about whether reality exists independently of observers.
There is a fundamental ontological divide between realism and constructivism in social science research.
words.englishDefinition: relating to the nature of being and the fundamental categories of existence

words.jpTranslation: オープンコーディング
exampleSentences: During open coding, the researcher labelled every distinct idea found in the interview transcripts.
Open coding is the first phase of grounded theory analysis, in which raw data are broken into discrete concepts.
distractors: 実在人物を題材にした小説
words.englishDefinition: an initial phase of qualitative analysis in which data are examined and labelled with conceptual categories

exampleSentences: The team had to operationalise 'academic success' as GPA plus course completion rate.
It is difficult to operationalise abstract concepts like wellbeing in a way that allows reliable measurement.
words.englishDefinition: to define an abstract concept in concrete, measurable terms suitable for empirical investigation

exampleSentences: Satisfaction ratings from 'very dissatisfied' to 'very satisfied' constitute an ordinal scale.
An ordinal scale indicates rank order but does not specify how much greater one value is than another.
words.englishDefinition: a measurement scale in which values can be ranked in order but the intervals between them are not necessarily equal

exampleSentences: The discovery of DNA's structure triggered a paradigm shift in how scientists understood heredity.
Kuhn argued that scientific progress is not gradual but occurs through sudden paradigm shifts.
words.englishDefinition: a fundamental change in the underlying assumptions and framework of a scientific discipline

words.jpTranslation: 参加型アクションリサーチ
exampleSentences: Participatory action research involved community members in designing solutions to local health problems.
The school adopted participatory action research to give students an active role in improving their environment.
words.englishDefinition: a collaborative research approach that engages participants as co-investigators to address real-world problems

exampleSentences: Findings were published in a peer-reviewed journal, lending them credibility within the scientific community.
Articles in a peer-reviewed journal are evaluated by independent experts before being accepted for publication.
words.englishDefinition: a scholarly publication in which submitted articles are evaluated by qualified experts in the field

exampleSentences: The phenomenological study explored how cancer patients experienced the transition to palliative care.
A phenomenological approach prioritises the lived experience of participants over objective measurements.
words.englishDefinition: relating to a research method that focuses on understanding subjects' conscious experiences and perceptions

exampleSentences: The pilot study revealed several ambiguous questions, which were revised before the main survey was launched.
Running a pilot study first saved time and resources by identifying methodological problems early.
words.englishDefinition: a small-scale preliminary study conducted to evaluate the feasibility and design of a larger investigation

exampleSentences: A positivist researcher believes that objective truths about the social world can be discovered through measurement.
The positivist tradition in psychology favours controlled experiments and statistical analysis over interpretation.
words.englishDefinition: relating to the view that valid knowledge is based only on observable, measurable facts

exampleSentences: The admissions test demonstrated high predictive validity by correlating strongly with first-year grades.
Predictive validity is essential for any assessment instrument used to forecast future performance.
words.englishDefinition: the extent to which a test or measure accurately predicts future outcomes or behaviours

exampleSentences: The historian relied on primary sources such as diaries, letters, and government records.
A laboratory notebook is a primary source because it records original experimental data directly.
words.englishDefinition: an original, firsthand document or data created at the time of study or by those directly involved

exampleSentences: Probability sampling ensures every member of the population has a known chance of being selected.
The national survey used stratified probability sampling to guarantee representation across all regions.
words.englishDefinition: a sampling method in which every unit in the population has a known, non-zero probability of selection

exampleSentences: Propensity score matching was applied to create comparable treatment and control groups from observational data.
Using a propensity score helps researchers reduce selection bias when random assignment is not possible.
distractors: オーケストレーション
words.englishDefinition: a statistical measure representing the probability of a unit being assigned to a particular treatment given observed characteristics

exampleSentences: Researchers used household income as a proxy measure for socioeconomic status in the analysis.
When the actual variable cannot be measured, a reliable proxy measure is the next best option.
words.englishDefinition: an indirect indicator used to represent a concept that cannot be directly observed or quantified

exampleSentences: Publication bias skews the scientific record because journals favour studies with positive results.
Meta-analyses must account for publication bias, as null results are often never submitted for review.
words.englishDefinition: the tendency for journals to publish studies with significant positive findings rather than null or negative results

exampleSentences: Qualitative coding involved assigning descriptive labels to segments of the interview transcripts.
Two independent researchers performed qualitative coding and then compared their category assignments.
words.englishDefinition: the process of labelling segments of qualitative data with descriptive or interpretive categories

exampleSentences: Random assignment of participants to groups is the hallmark of a true experimental design.
Without random assignment, systematic differences between groups may confound the results.
distractors: デュアルユース技術（軍民両用技術）
words.englishDefinition: the process of allocating participants to experimental conditions by chance to eliminate selection bias

exampleSentences: The randomised controlled trial is considered the most robust design for testing medical interventions.
A large randomised controlled trial confirmed that the vaccine significantly reduced hospitalisation rates.
words.englishDefinition: an experimental study in which participants are randomly allocated to receive or not receive an intervention

exampleSentences: Reflexivity required the ethnographer to continually question how her background shaped her interpretations.
In qualitative research, reflexivity involves acknowledging the researcher's influence on the study.
distractors: スノーボールサンプリング
words.englishDefinition: the practice of critically examining how a researcher's identity and perspective influence the research process

exampleSentences: Regression analysis revealed that study hours and prior knowledge together predicted 62% of exam variance.
Multiple regression analysis allows researchers to control for several variables simultaneously.
distractors: ハイパーパラメーター
words.englishDefinition: a statistical method for modelling the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more predictors

exampleSentences: The replication crisis in psychology prompted calls for greater transparency and open data practices.
Many landmark findings in social psychology failed to survive the replication crisis that emerged in the 2010s.
words.englishDefinition: an ongoing methodological crisis in science where many published results cannot be independently reproduced

exampleSentences: All studies involving human participants must receive clearance from the research ethics committee.
The research ethics committee required the team to revise their consent forms before granting approval.
words.englishDefinition: a body that reviews and approves research protocols to ensure they meet ethical standards for participant welfare

exampleSentences: The choice of research paradigm determines how data are collected, interpreted, and reported.
Positivism and interpretivism represent opposing research paradigms in the social sciences.
words.englishDefinition: a set of shared assumptions and values that guide how researchers in a field approach inquiry

exampleSentences: The research protocol specified the precise steps for sample collection and data analysis.
Deviating from the research protocol without approval can compromise the integrity of the entire study.
words.englishDefinition: a detailed plan describing the objectives, design, methods, and statistical procedures of a research project

exampleSentences: Blinding is used to minimise researcher bias during data collection and analysis.
The authors acknowledged researcher bias as a potential limitation in the discussion section of the paper.
words.englishDefinition: the influence of a researcher's preconceptions, beliefs, or expectations on the design, conduct, or interpretation of a study

exampleSentences: A retrospective study examined patients' medical records to identify risk factors associated with the illness.
Retrospective studies can be conducted quickly, but recall bias remains a significant methodological concern.
words.englishDefinition: a study that looks backward in time, examining existing data or records to investigate a phenomenon

exampleSentences: The paper was accepted only after rigorous peer review by three leading experts in the field.
Rigorous peer review is essential for maintaining the quality and credibility of published research.
distractors: 牧歌的表現、田園詩の伝統
words.englishDefinition: a thorough and demanding evaluation of a manuscript by qualified experts before publication

exampleSentences: Sample representativeness was a concern because all participants were drawn from a single urban university.
Stratified sampling was used to improve sample representativeness across age and gender subgroups.
words.englishDefinition: the degree to which the characteristics of a research sample mirror those of the broader population

exampleSentences: The researcher continued interviewing until reaching a saturation point where no new themes emerged.
In qualitative research, data collection ends at the saturation point rather than at a predetermined sample size.
words.englishDefinition: the point in qualitative research at which additional data collection produces no new categories or insights

exampleSentences: The debate about methodology has been a central feature of scholarly discourse in sociology for decades.
His article made a valuable contribution to scholarly discourse on the ethics of genetic engineering.
words.englishDefinition: formal academic discussion and exchange of ideas within a field of study through publications and conferences

exampleSentences: Textbooks and review articles are secondary sources that interpret or summarise primary research.
Relying too heavily on secondary sources weakens an argument because the original data cannot be verified.
words.englishDefinition: a document that analyses, interprets, or summarises primary sources rather than providing original data

exampleSentences: The clinical trial was affected by selection bias because only the healthiest patients volunteered.
Researchers used random sampling to avoid selection bias and ensure a representative group.
words.englishDefinition: a systematic error that arises when the sample chosen is not representative of the intended population

words.jpTranslation: スノーボールサンプリング
exampleSentences: Snowball sampling was used to recruit participants from a hard-to-reach undocumented migrant community.
Each participant in the snowball sampling approach referred the researcher to two or three additional contacts.
words.englishDefinition: a non-probability sampling method in which existing participants recruit further participants from their networks

exampleSentences: Statistical significance alone does not imply that a finding is practically meaningful or important.
The result reached statistical significance at the p < 0.01 level, indicating a very low probability of chance.
words.englishDefinition: a measure indicating that the likelihood of an observed result occurring by chance is below a specified threshold

exampleSentences: The structured interview used the same set of questions with every participant to ensure comparability.
A structured interview sacrifices depth for consistency, making it ideal for large quantitative surveys.
words.englishDefinition: a data-collection method in which the researcher asks all participants the same predetermined questions in the same order

words.jpTranslation: システマティックレビュー
exampleSentences: The systematic review synthesised evidence from 87 randomised controlled trials on dietary interventions.
A systematic review is more comprehensive and less prone to bias than a traditional narrative literature review.
distractors: 業務委託・アウトソーシング
words.englishDefinition: a rigorous, structured review of all existing literature on a specific research question using predefined criteria

exampleSentences: The study was grounded in a social constructivist theoretical framework drawn from Vygotsky's work.
A clearly articulated theoretical framework helps readers understand the assumptions underlying the research.
words.englishDefinition: a structured set of concepts and theories that provides a lens through which research is designed and interpreted

exampleSentences: The researcher stopped recruiting participants once theoretical saturation had been achieved in the analysis.
Theoretical saturation is reached when additional data no longer contribute new theoretical insights.
words.englishDefinition: the point in grounded theory research at which collecting more data does not add to the emerging theory

exampleSentences: Geertz advocated thick description to convey the full cultural context behind observed behaviours.
Thick description in ethnography goes beyond recording what happened to explaining its cultural meaning.
words.englishDefinition: a detailed account of observed behaviour that includes the context and meaning necessary for interpretation

exampleSentences: The author provided rich contextual detail to help readers assess the transferability of the findings.
Transferability in qualitative research is analogous to generalisability in quantitative studies.
words.englishDefinition: the extent to which qualitative research findings can be applied meaningfully to other contexts or populations

exampleSentences: Lincoln and Guba proposed four criteria for evaluating the trustworthiness of qualitative research.
Trustworthiness in qualitative studies encompasses credibility, transferability, dependability, and confirmability.
words.englishDefinition: the overall quality of qualitative research, encompassing its credibility, consistency, and applicability

exampleSentences: Library checkout records served as an unobtrusive measure of reading habits in the study.
Unobtrusive measures avoid the reactivity problem because participants are unaware of being observed.
words.englishDefinition: a data collection method that does not involve direct contact with participants and does not affect their behaviour

exampleSentences: In the cross-national study, the country rather than the individual was the unit of analysis.
Choosing the wrong unit of analysis can lead to ecological fallacy or other logical errors.
words.englishDefinition: the basic entity being examined in a research study, such as an individual, group, or organisation

exampleSentences: Researchers must demonstrate both reliability and validity before a measurement tool can be trusted.
The study's external validity was limited because all participants were first-year university students.
words.englishDefinition: the degree to which a research instrument measures what it is intended to measure

exampleSentences: Clear variable operationalisation is essential so that other researchers can replicate the procedure.
The paper devoted an entire section to variable operationalisation, defining each construct precisely.
words.englishDefinition: the process of defining variables in measurable terms so they can be observed and quantified in a study

exampleSentences: The researcher discussed the axiology underpinning the study and acknowledged her value-laden perspective.
Axiology addresses the role of values in research and whether inquiry can ever be truly value-free.
words.englishDefinition: the branch of philosophy concerned with the nature of value and how values influence research

exampleSentences: Bibliometrics was used to map the intellectual structure and evolution of the field over thirty years.
The h-index is one of the most widely used bibliometrics for evaluating a researcher's output.
words.englishDefinition: the statistical analysis of written publications to evaluate research output and scholarly influence

exampleSentences: Critical discourse analysis examined how news reports constructed narratives of social inequality.
She applied critical discourse analysis to reveal the power relations embedded in government policy texts.
words.englishDefinition: a method that analyses language to uncover how power, ideology, and social inequality are produced through texts

exampleSentences: The team used a deductive approach, deriving specific predictions from existing theory before collecting data.
A deductive approach begins with a theory and moves toward confirming or disconfirming it through evidence.
words.englishDefinition: a research strategy that starts with a theory or hypothesis and tests it against empirical observations

exampleSentences: The survey questions were reviewed by experts to ensure they had adequate face validity.
Although face validity is the weakest form, it is important that a test at least appears to measure the right thing.
distractors: アポフェニア（無関係なものに意味を見出す傾向）
words.englishDefinition: the extent to which a test appears on the surface to measure what it claims to measure

exampleSentences: The field experiment tested whether a simple nudge could increase voter turnout in real elections.
A field experiment offers higher external validity than a lab study at the cost of some experimental control.
words.englishDefinition: a study that applies experimental methods in a real-world setting rather than a controlled laboratory environment

words.jpTranslation: フォーカスグループ討議
exampleSentences: A focus group discussion with teachers revealed unexpected concerns about the new curriculum.
The focus group discussion generated rich qualitative insights that the survey data alone could not provide.
words.englishDefinition: a moderated group interview in which participants discuss a specific topic, generating qualitative data

exampleSentences: Hypothesis testing showed that the treatment group improved significantly more than the control group.
The first step in hypothesis testing is to formulate both the null and alternative hypotheses clearly.
words.englishDefinition: a statistical procedure that uses sample data to evaluate whether a hypothesis about a population can be supported

exampleSentences: The sociologist used an inductive approach, letting theory emerge organically from her ethnographic observations.
An inductive approach is especially appropriate when existing theory is limited or poorly suited to the context.
words.englishDefinition: a research strategy that builds theory upward from specific observations without a predetermined hypothesis

exampleSentences: Critical scholars question who controls knowledge production and whose perspectives are silenced.
Universities play a central role in knowledge production, shaping what society considers valid and important.
words.englishDefinition: the processes by which new understanding is generated, validated, and disseminated within a field

exampleSentences: The literature contained mixed evidence on whether social media use harms adolescent mental health.
Mixed evidence suggests that the effect of the intervention varies considerably across populations.
words.englishDefinition: a body of research that produces contradictory or inconsistent findings on the same question

exampleSentences: Using standardised checklists helped reduce observational bias during classroom behaviour assessments.
Observational bias may arise when a researcher unconsciously records events in ways that confirm expectations.
words.englishDefinition: a systematic distortion in data collection that results from the observer's expectations or knowledge influencing what is recorded

exampleSentences: Each chapter contributed evidence to support the overarching thesis of the dissertation.
The reviewer felt the essay lacked an overarching thesis that tied the individual arguments together.
words.englishDefinition: the central argument or claim that unifies and gives direction to an entire piece of scholarly work

words.jpTranslation: ピア・デブリーフィング
exampleSentences: Peer debriefing sessions with a colleague helped the researcher challenge her own interpretive assumptions.
Regular peer debriefing is recommended in qualitative research to enhance the credibility of findings.
words.englishDefinition: a qualitative research strategy in which a researcher discusses findings and interpretations with a knowledgeable peer to check for bias

exampleSentences: The researcher reflected on her positionality as a white middle-class woman studying a marginalised community.
Acknowledging positionality is an ethical requirement in qualitative and participatory research designs.
words.englishDefinition: the social and political context that shapes a researcher's identity, perspective, and relationship to the research subject

exampleSentences: The company's accounts receivable totaled over two million dollars at the end of the fiscal quarter.
Tight management of accounts receivable is essential for maintaining healthy cash flow.
words.englishDefinition: money owed to a business by its clients for goods or services already delivered

exampleSentences: Under accrual accounting, revenue is recorded when it is earned, not when cash is received.
Most large corporations are required to use accrual accounting rather than the cash basis method.
words.englishDefinition: a system of recording income and expenses when they are earned or incurred, regardless of when cash changes hands

exampleSentences: The fund manager claimed to generate consistent alpha by using proprietary quantitative models.
Investors seek alpha as a measure of a portfolio's performance relative to a benchmark index.
words.englishDefinition: the excess return of an investment above a benchmark, used to measure active management performance

exampleSentences: The startup secured seed funding from an angel investor who had previously built a successful fintech company.
An angel investor typically provides capital in exchange for convertible debt or equity ownership.
words.englishDefinition: a wealthy individual who provides early-stage capital to startups in exchange for equity or convertible notes

exampleSentences: The fund reported an annualized return of 12 percent over the past five years.
Comparing investments on an annualized return basis allows for fair performance evaluation across different time horizons.
words.englishDefinition: an investment's gain or loss expressed as a yearly percentage, regardless of the actual holding period

exampleSentences: Banks pool mortgages and car loans to create asset-backed securities that are sold to institutional investors.
The collapse of the housing market severely impacted the value of many asset-backed securities in 2008.
words.englishDefinition: a financial instrument whose value and income payments are derived from a pool of underlying assets such as loans or receivables

exampleSentences: Analysts scrutinized the company's balance sheet to assess its long-term financial stability.
A healthy balance sheet with low debt and ample cash reserves signals investor confidence.
words.englishDefinition: a financial statement listing a company's assets, liabilities, and shareholders' equity at a specific point in time

exampleSentences: The central bank raised interest rates by 25 basis points in response to rising inflation.
Even a change of a few basis points in the yield can significantly affect bond valuations.
distractors: マネーロンダリング（資金洗浄）
words.englishDefinition: one hundredth of one percent (0.01%), used to describe small changes in interest rates or other financial measures

exampleSentences: A stock with a beta of 1.5 tends to move 50 percent more than the overall market.
Conservative investors prefer low-beta stocks that exhibit less volatility than the broader index.
words.englishDefinition: a measure of a security's price volatility relative to the overall market

exampleSentences: The bid-ask spread widens during periods of market turbulence, raising the cost of trading.
A narrow bid-ask spread is generally a sign of high liquidity in the market for that security.
words.englishDefinition: the difference between the highest price a buyer is willing to pay and the lowest price a seller will accept

exampleSentences: The stock was trading at twice its book value, suggesting the market expected strong future earnings.
Book value per share is calculated by dividing net assets by the number of outstanding shares.
words.englishDefinition: the net value of a company's assets as recorded on its balance sheet, after deducting liabilities

exampleSentences: The government announced plans to reduce the budget deficit through a combination of spending cuts and tax increases.
A persistent budget deficit can erode investor confidence and put downward pressure on the currency.
words.englishDefinition: the amount by which a government's expenditures exceed its revenues in a given fiscal period

exampleSentences: The issuer retired the callable bond early when interest rates fell, refinancing at a lower cost.
Investors demand a higher yield on callable bonds to compensate for the reinvestment risk they bear.
distractors: フローサイトメトリー
words.englishDefinition: a bond that the issuer has the right to redeem before its maturity date, usually when interest rates decline

exampleSentences: Regulators set minimum capital adequacy standards to ensure banks can absorb unexpected losses.
The Basel III framework tightened capital adequacy requirements for systemically important financial institutions.
words.englishDefinition: the requirement that a bank hold sufficient capital relative to its risk-weighted assets to remain solvent

exampleSentences: The CEO reviewed the firm's capital structure and decided to reduce debt by issuing new equity.
An optimal capital structure minimizes the weighted average cost of capital while preserving financial flexibility.
words.englishDefinition: the mix of debt and equity financing a company uses to fund its operations and investments

exampleSentences: The retailer shortened its cash conversion cycle by negotiating longer payment terms with suppliers.
A negative cash conversion cycle, common in e-commerce, means a company collects payment before paying its own bills.
words.englishDefinition: the time it takes a company to convert investments in inventory and other resources into cash from sales

exampleSentences: The borrower agreed to collateralize the loan with its portfolio of commercial real estate.
Banks are typically required to collateralize interbank lending during periods of financial stress.
words.englishDefinition: to pledge an asset as security for a loan or financial obligation

words.jpTranslation: コマーシャルペーパー
exampleSentences: Large corporations issue commercial paper to meet short-term funding needs at lower cost than bank borrowing.
During the financial crisis, the commercial paper market froze, threatening the liquidity of many firms.
distractors: テラコッタ、素焼き陶器
words.englishDefinition: short-term unsecured debt issued by corporations to finance immediate operating needs

exampleSentences: Albert Einstein allegedly called compounding the eighth wonder of the world, because returns build on previous returns.
Starting to invest early maximizes the benefits of compounding over a long time horizon.
words.englishDefinition: the process by which investment returns generate additional returns over time, accelerating wealth growth

exampleSentences: The central bank adopted a contractionary policy by raising rates to cool an overheating economy.
Contractionary policy typically reduces inflation but risks slowing economic growth and raising unemployment.
words.englishDefinition: monetary or fiscal measures designed to reduce spending and slow economic activity in order to control inflation

words.jpTranslation: 転換社債型新株予約権付社債
exampleSentences: The startup raised its pre-seed round through a convertible note that would convert to equity at Series A.
Convertible notes allow early investors to delay valuation discussions until the company has more traction.
words.englishDefinition: a short-term debt instrument that converts into equity upon a future financing round or trigger event

exampleSentences: Pension funds allocate a significant portion of assets to investment-grade corporate bonds for stable income.
The firm issued a ten-year corporate bond at a coupon rate of 4.5 percent to fund its expansion.
words.englishDefinition: a debt security issued by a corporation to raise capital, paying fixed interest to bondholders

exampleSentences: Projects with expected returns below the company's cost of capital destroy shareholder value.
A higher credit rating lowers a firm's cost of capital by reducing the interest it must pay on debt.
words.englishDefinition: the minimum return a company must earn on its investments to satisfy the expectations of its creditors and shareholders

words.jpTranslation: カウンターパーティーリスク
exampleSentences: Clearing houses were introduced to reduce counterparty risk in derivatives markets.
Banks assess counterparty risk before entering into large over-the-counter derivative contracts.
words.englishDefinition: the risk that the other party in a financial transaction will default on its obligations

exampleSentences: Small businesses were hit hardest by the credit crunch, as banks tightened lending standards sharply.
The central bank injected liquidity into the financial system to ease the credit crunch.
words.englishDefinition: a sudden reduction in the availability of credit from banks and other lenders, often occurring during financial downturns

words.jpTranslation: クレジット・デフォルト・スワップ
exampleSentences: Investors used credit default swaps to hedge against the risk of sovereign debt default in southern Europe.
The widespread use of credit default swaps amplified losses during the 2008 financial crisis.
words.englishDefinition: a financial contract that acts as insurance against the default of a borrower, paying out if the borrower fails to meet obligations

exampleSentences: A lender evaluates creditworthiness by examining the applicant's income, debt-to-income ratio, and payment history.
Maintaining strong creditworthiness allows corporations to access capital markets at favorable rates.
distractors: エンドツーエンド暗号化
words.englishDefinition: an assessment of how likely a borrower is to repay debts based on financial history and current conditions

exampleSentences: Cross-border transactions are subject to foreign exchange risk and varying regulatory requirements.
Blockchain technology has the potential to streamline cross-border transactions by reducing settlement times.
words.englishDefinition: a financial transaction involving parties in different countries, subject to currency and regulatory considerations

words.jpTranslation: クラウディングアウト
exampleSentences: Critics argued that heavy government borrowing would cause crowding out, raising interest rates for private firms.
Crowding out reduces the effectiveness of fiscal stimulus if public spending displaces private investment.
words.englishDefinition: the reduction in private investment that occurs when government borrowing raises interest rates and absorbs available capital

exampleSentences: A current ratio above 2.0 suggests the company has sufficient short-term assets to cover its immediate liabilities.
Analysts track the current ratio quarterly to detect early signs of liquidity stress.
words.englishDefinition: a liquidity ratio calculated by dividing current assets by current liabilities to assess short-term financial health

exampleSentences: The company breached a debt covenant when its interest coverage ratio fell below the required threshold.
Lenders include debt covenants to restrict borrower behavior and protect the value of their loans.
words.englishDefinition: a contractual condition attached to a loan requiring the borrower to meet certain financial or operational benchmarks

exampleSentences: The firm's high debt-to-equity ratio raised concerns about its ability to weather an economic slowdown.
Investors compare the debt-to-equity ratio across industry peers to gauge relative financial risk.
words.englishDefinition: a financial metric comparing a company's total debt to its shareholders' equity, indicating the degree of financial leverage

exampleSentences: Following the acquisition, management focused on deleveraging by directing free cash flow toward debt repayment.
Rapid deleveraging across the banking sector intensified the severity of the post-crisis recession.
words.englishDefinition: the process of reducing the amount of financial leverage or debt carried by a company or economy

words.jpTranslation: 需要側経済学（ケインズ経済学）
exampleSentences: Demand-side economics prescribes government stimulus spending to lift an economy out of recession.
Proponents of demand-side economics argue that boosting consumer spending is the fastest path to recovery.
words.englishDefinition: an economic theory holding that consumer and government spending drives economic growth and that stimulus can close output gaps

exampleSentences: Existing shareholders feared dilution when the company announced a large secondary offering.
Anti-dilution provisions in investment agreements protect early investors from losing ownership percentage.
distractors: カリキュラム・教育課程
words.englishDefinition: the reduction in existing shareholders' ownership percentage caused by the issuance of additional shares

words.jpTranslation: 割引キャッシュフロー
exampleSentences: The analyst used a discounted cash flow model to estimate the intrinsic value of the tech company.
Discounted cash flow analysis requires assumptions about future revenues and an appropriate discount rate.
words.englishDefinition: a valuation method that estimates the present value of an investment based on its projected future cash flows

exampleSentences: The gradual disinflation observed in late 2024 allowed policymakers to consider cutting interest rates.
Disinflation differs from deflation: prices are still rising, just at a slower pace.
words.englishDefinition: a slowdown in the rate of inflation, meaning prices still rise but at a decreasing pace

exampleSentences: Specialist investors acquired distressed assets from failed banks at deep discounts during the crisis.
Distressed asset funds aim to generate returns by restructuring or liquidating undervalued holdings.
words.englishDefinition: a financial asset or property trading significantly below its intrinsic value due to financial distress of the owner

exampleSentences: The board voted to increase the distributed profit per share following a record-breaking fiscal year.
Tax treatment of distributed profit varies significantly depending on a shareholder's jurisdiction.
words.englishDefinition: the portion of a company's earnings paid out to shareholders as dividends rather than retained in the business

exampleSentences: The conglomerate announced the divestiture of its consumer electronics division to refocus on core markets.
Regulators required a divestiture of overlapping business units as a condition for approving the merger.
words.englishDefinition: the sale or disposal of a business unit, subsidiary, or asset as part of a strategic restructuring

exampleSentences: Wages, salaries, and self-employment profits are all classified as earned income for tax purposes.
Many tax systems apply lower rates to capital gains than to earned income, sparking equity debates.
words.englishDefinition: income derived from active work, such as wages or self-employment, as distinguished from passive or investment income

exampleSentences: The superstar athlete earned economic rent because his unique talent commanded pay far above his opportunity cost.
Economists argue that taxing economic rent is efficient because it does not distort productive behavior.
words.englishDefinition: the income earned above the minimum required to keep a factor of production in its current use

exampleSentences: The property title search revealed an encumbrance in the form of an unpaid mortgage lien.
Investors require disclosure of any encumbrance on assets that are offered as collateral.
words.englishDefinition: a legal claim or restriction on an asset, such as a lien or mortgage, that can limit its transfer or reduce its value

exampleSentences: Enterprise value is often preferred over market capitalization because it accounts for a company's debt and cash.
The acquirer paid a premium over the target's enterprise value to secure strategic synergies.
words.englishDefinition: a measure of a company's total value, calculated as market capitalization plus debt minus cash and equivalents

exampleSentences: At the equilibrium price, the quantity of goods supplied matches the quantity demanded in the market.
A sudden supply disruption shifts the equilibrium price upward until demand adjusts or supply recovers.
words.englishDefinition: the price at which the quantity supplied in a market equals the quantity demanded, clearing the market

words.jpTranslation: エスクロー（第三者預託）
exampleSentences: The buyer deposited the purchase price in escrow pending satisfactory completion of due diligence.
In real estate transactions, an escrow account holds funds until all conditions of the sale are met.
words.englishDefinition: a neutral account held by a third party that releases funds or assets only when specified contractual conditions are fulfilled

exampleSentences: The government deployed expansionary fiscal policy through infrastructure spending to stimulate the sluggish economy.
Critics warned that prolonged expansionary fiscal policy could lead to unsustainable public debt levels.
distractors: デュアルユース技術（軍民両用技術）
words.englishDefinition: government measures such as increased spending or tax cuts intended to boost aggregate demand and accelerate growth

exampleSentences: The bond was issued at a discount but will be redeemed at face value upon maturity.
Interest payments on bonds are typically calculated as a percentage of the face value.
words.englishDefinition: the nominal or par value of a financial instrument, as stated on the certificate or contract, typically the amount repaid at maturity

exampleSentences: Economists measure GDP at factor cost by excluding indirect taxes and subsidies from the output figures.
Rising factor costs such as wages and raw materials squeezed profit margins across the manufacturing sector.
words.englishDefinition: the cost of production inputs including labor, capital, and land, excluding indirect taxes

exampleSentences: Financial contagion spread from the banking crisis in one country to equity markets around the world.
Policymakers designed cross-border coordination mechanisms to contain financial contagion during systemic crises.
distractors: グリーンウォッシング
words.englishDefinition: the rapid spread of financial distress from one market, institution, or country to others through interconnected channels

exampleSentences: Banks serve as financial intermediaries by channeling savings from depositors to borrowers who need capital.
The growth of fintech has disrupted traditional financial intermediaries by offering direct peer-to-peer lending.
words.englishDefinition: an institution that acts as a middleman between savers and borrowers, facilitating the flow of funds in the economy

exampleSentences: Governments worldwide deployed unprecedented fiscal stimulus packages to cushion the pandemic's economic blow.

words.englishDefinition: government spending increases or tax cuts designed to boost economic activity during a downturn

exampleSentences: Rent and executive salaries are fixed costs that must be paid regardless of the level of production.

words.englishDefinition: a cost that remains constant regardless of the volume of goods or services produced

exampleSentences: A low float can lead to high price volatility because relatively few shares are available for trading.
The company increased its public float by conducting a secondary offering of previously restricted shares.
words.englishDefinition: the number of a company's shares that are freely available for trading by the public on an exchange

exampleSentences: The central bank intervened in currency markets, drawing on its foreign exchange reserves to defend the peg.
Large foreign exchange reserves give a country greater resilience against speculative attacks on its currency.
words.englishDefinition: foreign currency assets held by a central bank to support its currency, pay international obligations, and stabilize exchange rates

exampleSentences: The exporter entered a forward contract to lock in the exchange rate and eliminate currency uncertainty.
Unlike futures, a forward contract is privately negotiated and not traded on a centralized exchange.
words.englishDefinition: a customized agreement to buy or sell an asset at a specified price on a future date, used primarily for hedging

exampleSentences: Countries with a free float allow market forces to determine the exchange rate without central bank intervention.
The IMF encouraged the government to move toward a free float to improve external competitiveness.
distractors: 業務委託・アウトソーシング
words.englishDefinition: an exchange rate regime in which a currency's value is determined entirely by supply and demand in the foreign exchange market

words.jpTranslation: フロントランニング（先行売買）
exampleSentences: The broker was fined for front-running client orders to profit from predictable price movements.
Regulations prohibiting front-running protect retail investors from exploitation by those with privileged information.
words.englishDefinition: the unethical or illegal practice of trading a security based on advance knowledge of a pending client order

exampleSentences: Money is fungible, meaning one dollar bill is interchangeable with any other dollar bill of the same denomination.
Unlike fungible commodities such as oil, real estate is not fungible because each property is unique.
words.englishDefinition: interchangeable with other items of the same type, so that one unit can be substituted for another

exampleSentences: The software company boasted a gross margin exceeding 80 percent thanks to low marginal delivery costs.
A declining gross margin can signal rising input costs or increasing pricing pressure from competitors.
distractors: リモートワーク・在宅勤務
words.englishDefinition: the percentage of revenue remaining after deducting the direct costs of producing goods or services

words.jpTranslation: ヘッジング（リスク回避）
exampleSentences: The airline used commodity futures for hedging against sudden spikes in jet fuel prices.
Hedging reduces potential losses but also limits upside gains, so it involves a trade-off.
words.englishDefinition: a risk management strategy using financial instruments to offset potential losses from adverse price movements

exampleSentences: Homeowners benefit from imputed income because they consume housing services without paying rent or reporting income.
Some countries tax imputed income from owner-occupied housing to achieve horizontal tax equity.
words.englishDefinition: a non-cash economic benefit attributed to an individual, such as the value of living rent-free in one's own home

exampleSentences: The income statement showed a sharp drop in operating profit despite robust revenue growth.
Investors examine the income statement to assess profitability trends and expense management.
words.englishDefinition: a financial report summarizing a company's revenues, costs, and net profit or loss over a specific period

words.jpTranslation: インデックス裁定取引
exampleSentences: High-frequency traders engage in index arbitrage to exploit pricing gaps between index futures and the underlying stocks.
Index arbitrage helps keep futures prices aligned with the fair value of the index components.
words.englishDefinition: simultaneous buying and selling of an index and its component securities to profit from temporary price discrepancies

exampleSentences: Most developed-country central banks have adopted inflation targeting to anchor expectations around a two-percent goal.
Critics of inflation targeting argue that it causes central banks to ignore asset price bubbles and financial stability risks.
words.englishDefinition: a monetary policy framework in which a central bank sets and publicly commits to a specific inflation rate as its primary goal

exampleSentences: After quality problems with its supplier, the manufacturer opted for insourcing to regain control of production.
The trend toward insourcing reflects concerns about supply chain resilience and intellectual property protection.
words.englishDefinition: the practice of handling business functions internally rather than contracting them out to external providers

words.jpTranslation: インタレスト・カバレッジ・レシオ
exampleSentences: A falling interest coverage ratio warned analysts that the company might struggle to service its debt.
Lenders often require borrowers to maintain a minimum interest coverage ratio as part of loan covenants.
distractors: 展覧会の内覧会、オープニング
words.englishDefinition: a financial ratio showing how many times a company's operating profit covers its interest expense

exampleSentences: The retailer improved its inventory turnover by adopting a just-in-time replenishment system.
High inventory turnover indicates efficient stock management, but it can also signal inadequate supply buffers.
words.englishDefinition: a ratio measuring how many times a company sells and replaces its inventory over a given period

exampleSentences: The SME used invoice discounting to unlock cash tied up in outstanding receivables without waiting for customers to pay.
Invoice discounting differs from factoring in that the business retains control of its sales ledger.
words.englishDefinition: a short-term borrowing facility that allows businesses to raise cash against their unpaid invoices

exampleSentences: The issuer priced the new bond offering at par with a five-year maturity and semiannual coupon payments.

words.englishDefinition: an entity, such as a corporation or government, that sells securities to raise funds from investors

exampleSentences: Keynesian economics argues that government intervention can stabilize an economy during a recession.
The resurgence of Keynesian economics during the pandemic justified large-scale government rescue packages.
words.englishDefinition: an economic theory advocating that aggregate demand, influenced by government and public sector action, drives economic output

exampleSentences: Unemployment is considered a lagging indicator because it typically rises after the economy has already contracted.

words.englishDefinition: an economic measure that changes after the overall economy has begun a new trend, confirming rather than predicting shifts

exampleSentences: New housing permits are a leading indicator of future construction activity and broader economic health.

exampleSentences: The central bank acted as lender of last resort, providing emergency liquidity to prevent a bank run.

words.englishDefinition: a central bank's role of providing emergency loans to solvent but illiquid financial institutions to prevent systemic collapse

exampleSentences: The two companies signed a letter of intent before beginning the formal due diligence process for the merger.
Although typically non-binding, a letter of intent signals serious commitment from both parties.
words.englishDefinition: a document outlining the terms of a proposed agreement between parties before a formal contract is executed

exampleSentences: The bank placed a lien on the borrower's property as collateral for the commercial loan.

words.englishDefinition: a legal right or claim against a debtor's property, allowing a creditor to retain it as security until a debt is paid

words.jpTranslation: マクロプルーデンシャル（金融システム全体の健全性維持）
exampleSentences: Regulators adopted macroprudential tools such as countercyclical capital buffers to curb excessive credit growth.

words.englishDefinition: relating to policies aimed at ensuring the stability of the entire financial system rather than individual institutions

exampleSentences: The firm expanded output as long as the revenue from each additional unit exceeded its marginal cost.

words.englishDefinition: the additional cost incurred to produce one more unit of a good or service

exampleSentences: Profit is maximized at the output level where marginal revenue equals marginal cost.

exampleSentences: Market makers quote both buy and sell prices, providing continuous liquidity in securities markets.

words.englishDefinition: a financial firm that continuously quotes buy and sell prices for securities, profiting from the bid-ask spread

words.jpTranslation: マーケット・マイクロストラクチャー
exampleSentences: Researchers in market microstructure study how trading rules and mechanisms affect price discovery.

words.englishDefinition: the study of the mechanisms and rules that govern how securities are traded and how prices are formed

exampleSentences: The bank's maturity mismatch exposed it to liquidity risk when depositors withdrew funds unexpectedly.

words.englishDefinition: the gap between the short-term liabilities and longer-term assets held by a financial institution, creating potential liquidity risk

exampleSentences: The merchant bank advised the family business on its initial public offering and post-IPO investor relations.
Merchant banks traditionally specialize in trade financing, underwriting, and corporate advisory services.
distractors: フィンテック（金融テクノロジー）
words.englishDefinition: a bank that provides capital-markets services, M&A advisory, and trade financing rather than retail banking

words.jpTranslation: メザニン・ファイナンス
exampleSentences: The buyout was structured with senior debt, mezzanine financing, and equity to optimize returns for each investor tier.

words.jpTranslation: 金融政策の波及メカニズム
exampleSentences: The effectiveness of rate cuts depends on the strength of monetary transmission through the banking system.

exampleSentences: The board approved the capital project because its net present value was strongly positive at the given discount rate.

exampleSentences: The central bank conducted an open market operation by purchasing government bonds to inject liquidity.

exampleSentences: Overhead absorption rates are used to allocate fixed manufacturing costs to each unit produced.

exampleSentences: The bond will be redeemed at par value of one thousand dollars per unit when it matures in ten years.

exampleSentences: The manufacturing plant had a payback period of four years, after which it would generate pure profit.

exampleSentences: The e-commerce platform integrated a payment gateway to securely process credit card transactions.

words.englishDefinition: a technology service that authorizes and processes electronic payment transactions between merchants and customers

words.jpTranslation: ポートフォリオのリバランス

words.jpTranslation: プリンシパル・エージェント問題
exampleSentences: The principal-agent problem arises when executives make decisions that benefit themselves rather than shareholders.

words.jpTranslation: 予測財務諸表（プロフォーマ）

exampleSentences: The Volcker Rule restricted banks from engaging in proprietary trading to reduce systemic risk.

exampleSentences: Time deposits and money market funds are classified as quasi-money because they are highly liquid but not legal tender.

exampleSentences: The central bank raised the repo rate to curb inflationary pressures stemming from strong domestic demand.

exampleSentences: Many overseas citizens cast their votes using an absentee ballot.

exampleSentences: The country's accession to the EU was celebrated by its citizens.

exampleSentences: Voters were asked to declare their party affiliation before the primary.

exampleSentences: The government decided to allocate more funds to public housing.

exampleSentences: An international arbitrator was appointed to resolve the trade dispute.

exampleSentences: Citizens collected enough signatures to put the ballot initiative to a public vote.

exampleSentences: Freedom of the press serves as a bulwark against government overreach.

exampleSentences: Programs that promote civic engagement among youth are essential for democracy.

words.englishDefinition: an imaging technique that vitrifies biological samples at cryogenic temperatures and uses electron beams to reveal macromolecular structures at near-atomic resolution

exampleSentences: Dark current in photodetectors sets a noise floor that limits sensitivity in low-light astronomical imaging.
Cooling a CCD sensor reduces dark current, which is why astrophotography cameras are often thermoelectrically cooled.
words.englishDefinition: the small electric current that flows through a photodetector or solar cell in the absence of light, due to thermal excitation of charge carriers

exampleSentences: Data augmentation techniques such as random cropping and flipping increase the effective size of image training sets.

words.englishDefinition: the practice of artificially increasing a training dataset by applying transformations—such as rotation, scaling, or noise addition—to existing samples

exampleSentences: Decoherence occurs when a quantum system interacts with its environment, causing it to lose its quantum properties.

distractors: キーボードショートカット
words.englishDefinition: the process by which a quantum system loses its quantum superposition and entanglement through interaction with the surrounding environment

exampleSentences: The capacitor stores charge by maintaining an electric field across a thin dielectric layer between two conductive plates.
High-k dielectric materials allow transistor gates to be made thinner without increasing leakage current.
words.englishDefinition: an electrical insulator that can be polarized by an applied electric field, used to store energy in capacitors and reduce leakage in transistors

exampleSentences: Diffraction of X-rays by crystalline materials reveals the arrangement of atoms within the crystal lattice.

distractors: 回復力・レジリエンス
words.englishDefinition: the bending and spreading of waves around obstacles or through openings, occurring when the wavelength is comparable to the size of the obstacle

exampleSentences: The discrete Fourier transform converts a sampled signal from the time domain into its frequency components.
Audio compression algorithms apply the discrete Fourier transform to separate and discard inaudible frequency components.
words.englishDefinition: a mathematical transform that decomposes a finite sequence of sampled values into its constituent frequency components

words.jpTranslation: ドーピング（半導体への不純物添加）
exampleSentences: Doping silicon with phosphorus atoms introduces extra electrons, creating an n-type semiconductor used in transistors.

words.englishDefinition: the intentional introduction of impurity atoms into a semiconductor material to modify its electrical properties

exampleSentences: Electric vehicle manufacturers invest heavily in body design to minimize the drag coefficient and extend driving range.

words.englishDefinition: a dimensionless number that quantifies the aerodynamic resistance of an object moving through a fluid

words.jpTranslation: デュアルユース技術（軍民両用技術）
exampleSentences: Encryption software is a dual-use technology that protects civilian communications but can also shield criminal activity.

words.englishDefinition: technology that has both legitimate civilian applications and potential military or other harmful uses

exampleSentences: In quantum mechanics, the eigenvalues of the energy operator correspond to the allowed energy levels of a particle.

words.englishDefinition: a scalar value associated with a linear transformation of a vector space, such that a corresponding non-zero eigenvector is only scaled, not rotated

exampleSentences: Green hydrogen is produced by electrolysis of water powered by renewable electricity, splitting water into hydrogen and oxygen.

words.englishDefinition: the decomposition of a chemical compound by passing an electric current through a solution or molten state

words.jpTranslation: エレクトロマイグレーション
exampleSentences: Electromigration gradually displaces metal atoms in narrow interconnects, eventually causing circuit failure in advanced chips.

words.englishDefinition: the ratio of radiation emitted by a surface to that emitted by a perfect blackbody at the same temperature

exampleSentences: Dissolving ammonium nitrate in water is an endothermic process that absorbs heat, which is why the solution feels cold.

words.englishDefinition: describing a chemical reaction or process that absorbs heat from its surroundings, causing a temperature decrease

exampleSentences: Quantum entanglement links two particles so that measuring one instantly determines the state of the other, regardless of distance.

words.englishDefinition: a quantum mechanical phenomenon in which two or more particles become correlated so that the state of each cannot be described independently

exampleSentences: The second law of thermodynamics states that entropy in an isolated system never decreases spontaneously.
In information theory, entropy measures the average uncertainty—or information content—of a random variable.
words.englishDefinition: a measure of the disorder or randomness in a system; in thermodynamics, the unavailability of energy; in information theory, the uncertainty of a message source

exampleSentences: Epitaxial growth deposits single-crystal semiconductor layers atom by atom onto a substrate wafer, producing highly pure films.

words.englishDefinition: the process of depositing a thin, crystalline layer on a substrate so that the new layer mirrors the atomic structure of the underlying material

exampleSentences: Euler's number, approximately 2.718, is the base of the natural logarithm and appears throughout calculus and finance.

words.englishDefinition: describing a chemical reaction or process that releases heat into the surroundings, causing a temperature increase

exampleSentences: The nuclear plant's fail-safe design ensures that a loss of power automatically inserts control rods to shut down the reactor.

exampleSentences: A Faraday cage blocks external electromagnetic fields, protecting sensitive instruments from radio frequency interference.

exampleSentences: Feedback inhibition prevents overproduction of an amino acid by allowing the end product to switch off the first enzyme in its own synthesis pathway.

exampleSentences: Industrial fermentation is used to produce antibiotics, biofuels, and food ingredients at large scale using microorganisms.

words.englishDefinition: a metabolic process in which microorganisms convert sugars into acids, gases, or alcohols in the absence of oxygen

exampleSentences: Engineers use finite element analysis to simulate stress distribution in a bridge before a single piece of steel is cut.

exampleSentences: Uranium-235 is a fissile material that sustains a chain reaction when bombarded with neutrons of the right energy.

words.englishDefinition: a material capable of sustaining a nuclear chain reaction, because its nuclei can be split by neutrons of any energy

words.jpTranslation: フローサイトメトリー
exampleSentences: Flow cytometry can count and sort thousands of cells per second based on their size and fluorescence markers.

exampleSentences: Deep-space probes use forward error correction to recover data even when cosmic noise corrupts a significant fraction of bits.

exampleSentences: Fourier analysis decomposes a complex sound wave into its constituent pure tones, enabling equalizers and audio filters.

exampleSentences: Hydrogen fuel cells convert chemical energy directly into electricity through an electrochemical reaction, emitting only water.

exampleSentences: A hydroxyl functional group makes an organic molecule more water-soluble and reactive toward esterification.

words.englishDefinition: a specific group of atoms within a molecule that is responsible for its characteristic chemical reactions and properties

words.jpTranslation: 亜鉛めっきする、奮い立たせる
exampleSentences: Steel structures are galvanized by coating them with zinc to prevent corrosion in outdoor environments.

exampleSentences: Gradient descent iteratively adjusts model parameters in the direction that most reduces the loss function.

words.englishDefinition: an iterative optimization algorithm that adjusts parameters by moving in the direction of the steepest decrease in a function

exampleSentences: Graphene, a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice, is the strongest material ever measured.

exampleSentences: Carbon-14 has a half-life of about 5,730 years, making it useful for dating organic materials up to roughly 50,000 years old.

exampleSentences: The Hall effect generates a transverse voltage when a current-carrying conductor is placed in a perpendicular magnetic field.

exampleSentences: The satellite's payload includes instruments for measuring atmospheric composition and surface temperature.

words.jpTranslation: 重ね書き羊皮紙、痕跡の残る物

exampleSentences: The recurring motif of water in the novel symbolizes both life and destruction.

words.jpTranslation: 展覧会の内覧会、オープニング

exampleSentences: The retrospective exhibition surveyed the artist's entire oeuvre across five decades.

words.jpTranslation: 真実らしさ、リアリティ

exampleSentences: The publication received the imprimatur of the leading literary society.

exampleSentences: A hermeneutic approach to the text considers the author's historical context.

exampleSentences: The bronze sculpture had acquired a rich green patina over centuries of exposure.

words.jpTranslation: 舞台演出、映画の場面設定

words.jpTranslation: ライトモチーフ、繰り返し主題

words.jpTranslation: レチタティーヴォ、語り歌い

words.jpTranslation: 多声音楽、ポリフォニー

exampleSentences: The cellos provide a melancholic counterpoint to the bright melody of the violins.

exampleSentences: The brass band played a solemn dirge as the funeral procession wound through the streets.

words.jpTranslation: 透かし細工、精巧な装飾

exampleSentences: Dürer's engravings are celebrated for their extraordinary precision and detail.

exampleSentences: Hieronymus Bosch's Garden of Earthly Delights is among the most famous triptychs in art history.

exampleSentences: The story of Newton discovering gravity from a falling apple may be apocryphal.

words.jpTranslation: 実在人物を題材にした小説

exampleSentences: The novel's palimpsestic narrative layers present-day events over buried colonial histories.

words.jpTranslation: 美的距離感、観客の感情的距離

words.englishDefinition: to remove oneself from a legal case due to a conflict of interest or partiality

exampleSentences: The firm hired a dedicated team to ensure ongoing regulatory compliance with financial laws.

words.englishDefinition: adherence to laws, regulations, and standards set by government authorities

exampleSentences: The governor granted a last-minute reprieve, halting the execution hours before it was to take place.

exampleSentences: The philosophy of retribution holds that punishment is a morally deserved response to crime.

exampleSentences: The government invoked sovereign immunity to prevent the lawsuit from proceeding in domestic courts.

words.englishDefinition: the legal doctrine that a government or state cannot be sued in its own courts without its consent

exampleSentences: Under stare decisis, lower courts are bound by precedents established by higher courts.
The principle of stare decisis promotes consistency and predictability in the law.
words.englishDefinition: the legal doctrine requiring courts to follow precedents established in previous decisions

exampleSentences: The eyewitness testimony was compelling but contradicted by the physical evidence.

words.englishDefinition: a formal statement made by a witness under oath in a court of law

exampleSentences: International cooperation is essential to combat the global scale of human trafficking networks.
Drug trafficking across international borders carries severe mandatory minimum sentences.
words.englishDefinition: the illegal trade of people or controlled substances across borders or regions

exampleSentences: The key informant was granted transactional immunity, protecting him from prosecution for all related offences.

exampleSentences: The will was contested on grounds of undue influence by a family member who had power of attorney.

exampleSentences: The military was accused of attempting to usurp the authority of the elected civilian government.

exampleSentences: Contracts entered into by minors are generally void ab initio in many legal systems.

exampleSentences: The attorney filed a writ of mandamus to compel the agency to release the requested documents.

exampleSentences: The therapist guided the patient through abreaction to release suppressed memories from childhood trauma.

exampleSentences: Children who learn affect regulation early in life tend to have healthier relationships as adults.

exampleSentences: Feeling agentive—having genuine control over decisions—is central to psychological wellbeing.

exampleSentences: Her ambivalence about leaving her hometown made it difficult to commit to the new job offer abroad.

words.jpTranslation: アポフェニア（無関係なものに意味を見出す傾向）
exampleSentences: Apophenia may explain why people see faces in clouds or connect unrelated events into conspiracy theories.

exampleSentences: Bowlby's attachment theory explains how early bonds with caregivers shape emotional development throughout life.

exampleSentences: Emotional attunement between parent and infant is crucial for the development of secure attachment.

exampleSentences: Research shows that autonomy support from parents fosters intrinsic motivation in children.

exampleSentences: Behavioral activation is a core component of cognitive behavioral therapy for depression.

exampleSentences: Fredrickson's broaden-and-build theory suggests that positive emotions expand our awareness and build lasting personal resources.

exampleSentences: Regular mindfulness practice has been shown to reduce symptoms of anxiety and depression in clinical populations.

exampleSentences: Mirror neurons are thought to underlie our ability to imitate actions and feel empathy for others' pain.
